title: Multi-task distribution learning approach to anomaly detection of operational states of wind turbines

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## LEARNING MULTIPLE DATA DISTRIBUTIONS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Vulnerability to Distribution Shift is a major challenge for real-world applications of Deep Learning. Distribution Shift occurs when the test data distribution is not identical to the training distribution. We present Multi-Distribution Learning, exploring the effectiveness of Data Augmentation to prepare for Distribution Shift. We use common Data Augmentations to simulate multiple data distributions and test how many of these distributions can be learned with a single model. We first illustrate the impact of adding the test distribution to the training distribution, such that it is no longer a Distribution Shift. We find success with pairs of distributions, however, the model is limited when attempting to learn 10 distributions simultaneously. In order to search for a subset of distributions that train well together, we use a Lookahead search derived from research in Multi-Task Learning. From this analysis, we select 4 distributions with high affinities to train together and 1 negative distribution to use for performance comparison. These experiments confirm that the accuracy of models evaluated on a test set included in the training distribution is much higher compared to test distributions not included in training. We evaluate our distribution grouping with Zero-Shot Distribution Inference to a held-out positive distribution and the held-out negative distribution. We compare this Zero-Shot Distribution Inference to a strong baseline of the RandAugment training scheme, as well as to a weak baseline of a model trained without Data Augmentation. We conclude by discussing directions for future work in using Data Augmentation to combat Distribution Shift.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Distribution Shift, defined as the performance on test distributions not identical to the training set, are the Achilles heel of Deep Learning. We propose a simple solution to use Data Augmentation for targeted Generalization, alleviating the problem of Distribution Shift. In training, we aim to cover as many data distributions as possible, such that a Distribution Shift is not completely novel to our model. We measure our models using the Average Distribution Accuracy and propose a new framework of Distribution Generalization Metrics. These performance scores are a weighted average of the model loss or accuracy, the number of distributions it can cover, and the diversity of these distributions.

We begin with two important questions for thinking about Distribution Shift. What are different data distributions? What does Distribution Shift in the real-world look like? A data distribution can be characterized based on the frequency of class labels. For example, the training set may contain 80% dogs and 20% cats, but the test distribution shifts to 40% dogs and 60% cats. In addition to distributions of labels, we can think about distributions of the inputs themselves. This is significantly harder to characterize due to the high-dimensional nature of raw inputs, such as pixel grids or word embeddings, or even intermediate vector representations from these sequential processing models.

The WILDS benchmark Koh et al. (2020) has been a very useful dataset to understand Distribution Shift in the real-world. Koh et al. collected examples of Distribution Shifts across applications in Wildlife Monitoring, Molecular Engineering, and Python Code Completion, to give a few examples. The authors characterize Distribution Shift into two categories, Subpopulation Shift and Domain Generalization. Subpopulation Shift is well illustrated by our previous example of a shifting frequency of cats and dogs. However, Subpopulation Shift can also refer to more high-level distributions, such as an increase in certain features of the input. Domain Generalization describes settings where the train and test domains are completely disjoint. For example, a sentiment classifier

Figure 1: The Multi-Distribution Learning framework evaluates how training with a data distribution helps with test performance on that distribution. The framework tests the limits of how many distributions we can learn with a single model.
trained on movie reviews and then tested on restaurant reviews Gururangan et al. (2020), or an image classifier trained on photorealistic images and tested on paintings Peng et al. (2019).

Another technique for studying Distribution Shift is through corruption tests. Datasets such as ImageNet-C Hendrycks & Dietterich (2019), ImageNet-Corrupted, are constructed by applying common Data Augmentations to the original ImageNet test set. Models that achieve high performance on ImageNet typically do not generalize to ImageNet-C as well. There are several other examples of these datasets, such as smaller-scale CIFAR-C and MNIST-C tests Mu & Gilmer (2019), ImageNet-P with more subtle corruptions, and Stylized-ImageNet Geirhos et al. (2019) which uses a style transfer algorithm to disentangle content from style in images. Corruption testing, achieved by applying Data Augmentation to an original test set, is an important lens for viewing the phenomenon of Distribution Shift. We explore how we can similarly use Data Augmentation to prepare for Distribution Shift and the limits of this technique.

Our objective is to use Data Augmentation to target the Generalization we want the model to achieve. This is a simple, but promising strategy to inject prior knowledge about how the distribution will shift. We believe these assumptions on the test data distribution are necessary to make progress, inspired by the following quote from Arjovsky (2019), “If the test data is arbitrary or unrelated to the training data, then generalization is obviously futile.” This quote supports the idea that we should make assumptions and bake in prior knowledge about the test distributions.

We begin our experiments by confirming that models which achieve high performance on an original test set will fail to generalize to the same test set corrupted with common data augmentations. This holds even for models trained with RandAugment Cubuk et al. (2020), a strong regularization strategy based on Data Augmentation. Our training strategy to achieve generalization to these test sets is illustrated in Figure 1. We apply N corruptions to the original data to form N training distributions. These N corruptions are additionally applied to the original test data to form an equivalent N test distributions. We report the average accuracy across the N test distributions. We begin by showing how this strategy increases the performance using N=2 augmentations.

We then scale our strategy up to learning N=10 distributions at once. We find that this requires much longer training times to achieve rivaling performance to single augmentation specializations trained with N=1. We borrow inspiration from research in Multi-Task Learning and use a Task Groupings algorithm Fifty et al. (2021) to search for distributions that train well together. We refer to these distributions as having a high affinity with each other. We use a Lookahead search to compare the performance of a proposed gradient update after training on a given distribution. Distributions are determined to train well together if the proposed update improves the performance across both test sets. We further attempt to disentangle the impact of repeating data in our distribution construction strategy by partitioning the CIFAR-10 set into 5,000 images each, to remove the confounding factor of repeating data. We find similar distribution groupings with and without repeating the original data.

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

We select 4 augmentations from our Lookahead search to train together. We additionally select 1 augmentation with a negative affinity for the sake of comparison. We find that the grouping achieves a much higher Average Distribution Score than the N=10 model trained with similar computation. We then generalize these experiments to a more realistic evaluation of Distribution Shift. We pose this as a problem of Zero-Shot Distribution Inference. We propose that groupings with a high affinity to the novel test distribution will perform better. We use a cross-validation analysis to iteratively pair 3 out of the 4 augmented distributions for training, evaluating the held-out distribution Zero-Shot. We find much better performance with this cluster compared to a strong RandAugment baseline, which is not completely Zero-Shot, as well as a weak baseline of a model trained without any Data Augmentation.

Our experiments utilize Data Augmentation to simulate diverse data distributions. We are currently limited to geometric transformations such as Rotations or Horizontal Flipping. However, we note that new frontiers in Data Augmentation such as CycleGAN Zhu et al. (2017) translations between domains or Text-to-Image generation Ramesh et al. (2021) should have a massive impact on this framework. We believe that Distribution Shift should be viewed similarly to Zero-Shot task inference commonly used in probing large models Liu et al. (2021). An interesting characteristic of Zero-Shot inference is the difference between massive models typically trained with less supervision than smaller, expert models. We view training on specialized distributions as analogous to supervised learning with particular tasks. Finally, we propose directions to extend our Distribution Generalization Score. Rather than the naive average accuracy, which we have reported in these experiments, we want a metric that rewards covering many distributions and the diversity of these distributions. We propose some ideas for measuring the diversity of these distributions.
In summary, we propose a framework of using Data Augmentation to anticipate and alleviate the challenges of Distribution Shift. Our contributions are as follows:

- We propose a new framework for Learning and Evaluating Multiple Data Distributions.

- We present a new metric for Distributional Generalization, the Average Distribution Score.
- We adapt a Task Grouping algorithm from Multi-Task Learning to study Multiple Distribution Learning.
- We present an analysis of Zero-Shot Distribution Inference, inspired by research on ZeroShot Task Inference.

## 2 RELATED WORK

## 2.1 DISTRIBUTION SHIFT

Understanding the nature of Generalization is one of the core goals of Artificial Intelligence Chollet (2019). Many recent works have characterized types of Generalization. These include Domain Transfer Peng et al. (2019), Compositional and Systematic Generalization Johnson et al. (2016), Concept Drift, or Robustness, to give a few examples. Further characterizations include In-Distribution versus Out-of-Distribution Generalization, viewing Generalization gaps in a similar lens as fine- and coarse-grained classification. From a practical perspective, Koh et al. (2020) have published the WILDS benchmark characterizing the generalization needed for many real-world applications. In this work, we propose a simple framework for studying the relationship between training and test distributions. Andreassen et al. (2021) and Wortsman et al. (2021) have also explored the relationship between Zero-Shot robustness and fine-tuned models. Regardless of the particular type of generalization, we can likely form Data Augmentations that communicate expected Distribution Shifts.

## 2.2 CORRUPTION TESTING

Many works have found that Deep Learning systems are vulnerable to corruption tests. These tests are generally head-scratching cases because they very closely resemble the original input. This defies a natural intuition about the data and requires thinking closely about the distributions of inputs. Corruption tests range from injecting adversarial noise maps to applying Data Augmentations, as we have done in this work. Hendrycks & Dietterich (2019) presented ImageNet-C, a dataset constructed

3

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

from a set of 75 Data Augmentations and ImageNet-P constructed from adversarial injections. Ovadia et al. (2019) used corruption sets to evaluate Uncertainty techniques in Deep Learning. **?** utilized style randomization to understand intrinsic properties of Convolutional Neural Networks, finding a texture bias.

## 2.3 DATA AUGMENTATION

Multi-Distribution Learning is a technique building on Data Augmentation for improving the Generalization of Deep Neural Networks. Many works have covered different Data Augmentation techniques across many domains. Some examples include Kernel Filters, Geometric Transformations, Random Erasing, Color Space Transformations, Mixing Images, Adversarial Training, Neural Style Transfer, and GAN Data Augmentation Shorten & Khoshgoftaar (2019). In addition to individual augmentations, recent works have explored new strategies for utilizing Data Augmentation. This includes application in contrastive losses in self-supervised learning such as SimCLR Chen et al. (2020), as well as consistency losses in Unsupervised Data Augmentation Xie et al. (2020). Sinha et al. (2021) have additionally explored utilizing Data Augmentations that intentionally create Out-of-Distribution data. Our work is similar in that we are looking for new ways to utilize Data Augmentation in Deep Learning.

## 3 EXPERIMENTS

Our experiments report training and evaluation strategies for the ResNet152V2 He et al. (2016) on the CIFAR-10 dataset. The experiments are implemented using the Keras framework. The experiments are run on an NVIDIA A100 GPU in a Google Colab Pro+ runtime, with a maximum training time of roughly 20 hours. We experiment with up to 10 common Data Augmentations used for image data: Rotation, Translation, Crop, Cutout, Bright, Dark, Gamma Contrast, Horizontal Flip, Vertical Flip, and Jigsaw. These augmentations are implemented using the imgaug open-source library. Following is a quick description of how these augmentations transform the original data distribution.

The Rotation augmentation rotates an image along polar coordinates between 0 to 360 degrees. The Translation augmentation shifts an image along the x and y axes. This is done by moving pixels over/up by x pixels and adding 0s to displaced values. The Crop augmentation selects a subset of the original image and then resizes the subset to the original resolution with bilinear interpolation. The Cutout augmentation selects patches of the image and replaces the patch with 0 values. The Bright augmentation adds k to each pixel value in the image, essentially pushing it closer to 255 (white). The Dark augmentation inversely subtracts k to each pixel value in the image, essentially pushing it closer to 0 (black). The Gamma Contrast augmentation alters the distribution of pixel values to a smaller window between 0 and 255. The Horizontal Flip (LR Flip) augmentation mirrors the original image along the y-axis. The Vertical Flip (UD Flip) augmentation inversely mirrors the original image along the x-axis. The Jigsaw augmentation divides the image into n pixel windows and shuffles their location in the image.

As a quick summary of our experiments, Training with 2 Distributions characterizes the problem of Distribution Shift and the simple solution of adding the test distribution to the training set. The model is not training with the test set itself, just the underlying distribution that will be encountered in-the-wild. We simulate the different distributions through Data Augmentations. We then scale this up to N=10 distributions in training. Lookahead Search to Group Training Distributions utilizes Lookahead analysis to find distributions that complement each other during training. Results of Grouping Training Distributions compares the Average Distribution Score with the grouped distributions to N=1 and N=10 distribution training strategies. Table 4 illustrates the Zero-Shot Distribution Inference of the grouping with a strong RandAugment baseline and a weak baseline of training with No Data Augmentation.

## 3.1 TRAINING WITH 2 DISTRIBUTIONS

We begin by highlighting the problem of Distribution Shift, reproducing it in our experimental setup, and conducting simple experiments within the framework described in Figure 1. As shown in Table 1, when trained without any Data Augmentation the model performs much worse on the Rotation

Table 1: The efficacy of training with a specialized distribution. The bolded values indicate the maximum accuracy achieved on each data distribution. The bolded values indicate the maximumm accuracy for each data distribution.
Table 2: Learning curves for Multi-Distribution Learning of 10 Augmentations. The schematic of this training strategy is shown in Figure 1.

and Jigsaw distributions compared to the original CIFAR-10 data. Even though the RandAugment training scheme dramatically improves performance on the original set, it is still susceptible to these augmentation derived distribution shifts. We then report the efficacy of specialized training on the distribution used in testing, such that it is no longer a Distribution Shift. We shift our aim to learning to specialize on multiple distributions in training. We consider two high-level schema for doing this. The first of which is Sequential Training (abbreviated as Seq in Table 1). Sequential Training refers to first learning the Rotation distribution and then learning the Jigsaw distribution. We encounter an interesting case of Catastrophic Forgetting where performance on the previously learned distribution quickly drops as it shifts focus to another data distribution. We find a better result with Alternating Training (abbreviated as Alt in Table 1). In Alternating Training we switch between each training distribution every epoch, rather than after convergence. We additionally test sharing a base feature extractor between each distribution and using separate classification heads to model each distribution (abbreviated as Alt-SH in Table 1). We do not find much difference with separate heads compared to fine-tuning the entire model. We continue with Alternating Training throughout the rest of the experiments.

## 3.2 TRAINING WITH 10 DISTRIBUTIONS

Following the success of learning 2 distributions in training, we set our sights on increasing the number of learned data distributions. Table 2 illustrates the learning curve of 10 data distributions. We find that it takes much longer for this strategy to achieve a strong Average Augmentation Score. However, after 1,000 epochs (roughly 20 hours of training with our computing setup described previously), the model achieves a fairly strong Average Augmentation Score with small variance and no significant minimum or maximum outliers in the accuracy scores. Throughout training the Rotation, Translation, and Crop Augmentations have the lowest performance, whereas Vertical and Horizontal Flipping (shown as UD Flip and LR Flip) have the highest score. At the end of training the multi-distribution learning model achieves a nearly identical score on the Horizontal Flip as the Original Test set, it could be interesting future work to see if the model has achieved invariance to this distribution shift in the prediction and representation spaces. We find more interesting details about the Vertical Flip augmentation in our analysis of Lookahead Search.

Figure 2: This table shows the performance difference by taking the proposed gradient update on the y-axis on the test distributions on the x-axis. In this index: Rotate = 0, Translate = 1, Crop = 2, Cutout = 3, Bright = 4, Dark = 5, Gamma Contrast = 6, LR Flip = 7, UD Flip = 8, and Jigsaw = 9.

## 3.3 LOOKAHEAD SEARCH TO GROUP TRAINING DISTRIBUTIONS

Due to the long training time of learning 10 distributions at once, we aim to find a subset of distributions that have a faster learning curve and higher performance. We adapt the Task Groupings algorithm Fifty et al. (2021) to search for this subset. This algorithm uses a Lookahead search to compute what the change in performance on other tasks, or distributions in our example, will change. For example, in Multi-Task Learning the Task Groupings algorithm computes the change in performance on Semantic Segmentation after taking a gradient update on Object Detection. We similarly compute the change in performance on the Translation distribution after taking a gradient step on the Rotated training set.

The results of the Distribution Grouping analysis are shown in Figure 2. The x-axis is the evaluated distribution and the y-axis is the distribution that proposes a gradient update. The augmentations are indexed according to their position in the following list: Rotate = 0, Translate = 1, Crop = 2, Cutout = 3, Bright = 4, Dark = 5, Gamma Contrast = 6, LR Flip = 7, UD Flip = 8, and Jigsaw = 9. We find a strong outlier result where a proposed gradient update on Vertical Flipping (UD Flip) causes a significant drop in performance across the other augmentations. This likely explains the optimization difficulty behind learning all 10 distributions simultaneously. The second strongest negative result is the impact of updating on Bright and evaluating on the Dark test distribution. We leave it to future work to explore if the inverse augmentations of increased and decreased Brightness have opposite gradient directions.

From Figure 2, we propose to group Rotation, Translation, Dark, and Jigsaw to form our subset. In order to further understand the impact of a gradient update on one distribution to the performance of another, we avoid repeating data. For example, the rotation distribution still has roughly the same image as the translation distribution in Figure 2. The right hand side of Figure 3 illustrates the lookahead updates when we split the data such that no distribution has the same original CIFAR-10 image. We find a similar grouping analysis with and without repeating data.

## 3.4 RESULTS OF GROUPING TRAINING DISTRIBUTIONS

We analyze the performance of our distribution grouping on learning the training distributions and zero-shot inference to novel distributions. Table 3 reports enumerating through 3 of the 4 selected distributions for training. We encountered an optimization difficulty with the Rotate, Translate, Jigsaw (abbreviated as RTJ in Table3) that achieves low performance. However, the other 3 clusters outperform the 10 Augmentation scheme other than the Jigsaw test. We find an interesting division between distributions that benefit from specialization (Rotation and Translation) and others that benefit from the ensemble (Darker and Jigsaw). This could be an interesting property for the design of ensembles and specialist distribution models that we leave for future work.

The ultimate goal of learning multiple data distributions is to lessen the decrease in accuracy when faced with Distribution Shift. We test how the grouped distributions help with Zero-Shot Distribution Inference through a cross-validation analysis within the group. For example, we hold-out the Rotation distribution and train with Translate, Darker, and Jigsaw. The entires for 3 Augmentations in Table 4 reflect the Zero-Shot accuracy without training on that augmented distribution. For Rotation and Translation, we find a very strong Zero-Shot generalization with the group. As mentioned in Table 3, we ran into optimization difficulties with our run leaving out the Darker distribution and it achieves a very low accuracy. We found a similar performance on Zero-Shot Jigsaw generalization. We held-out Gamma Contrast as an example of a distribution with a negative affinity to the group. We report the best Zero-Shot accuracy on Gamma Contrast, supporting our analysis that the negative augmentation does not benefit from this particular cluster of training distributions. We compare this with the RandAugment model reported in Table 1, which achieves 83.8% accuracy on the original CIFAR-10 test set. Although RandAugment has never seen any of these distributions in isolation, it has seen compositions of them such as rotation, then translation, then darker applied to a single image. Thus, the RandAugment baseline is not truly Zero-Shot. The No Augmentation baseline highlights the poor performance when faced with Distribution Shift. In both RandAugment and No Augmentation we see the lowest performance decrease with the weakest augmentation, Darker. This highlights the need to develop distribution distance scores to weight the performances.

## 4 DISCUSSION

## 4.1 FUTURE DIRECTIONS FOR SIMULATING DATA DISTRIBUTIONS

Our experiments tested simple data augmentations to simulate distribution shifts. We have used the terms “augmentations” and “distributions” interchangeably to propose their similarities. We

believe techniques such as CycleGAN Zhu et al. (2017) and DALL-E Ramesh et al. (2021) will allow us to control Domain Generalization with Data Augmentation-style interfaces. For example, the iWildlife-monitoring camera in the WILDS benchmark encounters a new location for a wildelife monitoring camera at test time. A CycleGAN may be able to map the existing training data into this new location, further guided with text interfaces from DALL-E. We propose that we can train with this inferred novel distribution to perform better in the Distribution Shift.

## 4.2 ZERO-SHOT DISTRIBUTION INFERENCE

Few-Shot Learning describes learning a new task with a few examples, hopefully achieved by leveraging information from previously learned tasks. Zero-Shot Learning refers to a more extreme case in which the model performs a new task, but no additional learning is allowed. We can similarly view Distribution Shifts as having a few examples from the new distribution or having to perform Zero-Shot task inference. Another interesting trend in Few- and Zero-Shot learning is the efficacy of larger-scale models. Models such as GPT-3 can be guided with prompts to perform novel language tasks. It remains to be seen whether collections of expert models assigned to their respective inferences will outperform a single monolithic model. Even if it becomes unnecessary to train expert models for collections of tasks, or distributions as we have argued, understanding novel tasks will still be useful for evaluation.

## 4.3 DISTRIBUTION GENERALIZATION SCORE

Our experiments report a simple average augmentation accuracy. For future work we intend to explore metrics that better capture the diversity of a model’s generalization ability. We intend to weight the average accuracies by the number of evaluated distributions and the distance between these distributions. The distance between distributions is a challenging quantity to measure. One solution, similar to ideas in the automated evaluation of generated images such as the Inception Score, could be to embed each distribution into the feature space of a pre-trained classifier and use these vector representations to compute distances. A strong Distribution Generalization Score should also be correlated with performance on Zero-Shot Distribution Inference.

## 5 CONCLUSION

In Conclusion, we have presented Multi-Distribution Learning, a framework to study the impact of training on potential test distributions. This is a simple framework to leverage the prior knowledge about how a data distribution is likely to change in deployment. Key to our framework are the number of distributions used in training and the metric used to score the diversity of distribution performance. We have presented several experiments that highlight the challenge of learning many distributions at once. We have additionally presented a technique to group together distributions for

training. Finally, we propose framing the problem of Distribution Shift as analogous to Zero-Shot Task Inference. We believe that rapid advances in Data Augmentation and Generative Modeling will have a large impact on this research direction.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a timely and conceptually interesting framework—using data augmentation to simulate and prepare for distribution shifts—by drawing connections between multi-task learning, zero-shot inference, and robustness. It demonstrates a clear experimental pipeline and identifies meaningful patterns in distribution affinities, particularly the negative interference of certain augmentations. The adaptation of lookahead search for grouping distributions is a notable methodological choice that aligns with recent trends in structured training.

Overview issues:

- Unclear and inconsistent definition of 'data distribution': The paper uses 'data distribution' interchangeably to refer to label marginals, input corruptions, and augmented versions of data, without formally distinguishing between them. For instance, Section 2.1 equates distribution shift with class imbalance, while Section 3 experiments treat geometric or color transformations as distinct distributions. This conflation undermines the clarity of claims: are the models learning to generalize across semantic shifts or superficial corruptions? Readers will struggle to assess real-world relevance because the term 'distribution' lacks a consistent operational definition. The authors should disentangle these notions—perhaps by categorizing shifts into semantic, geometric, and photometric types—and justify which are most critical for generalization.

- Overstated zero-shot inference claims due to baseline leakage: The paper positions its grouped distribution training as enabling zero-shot inference on novel distributions, but the RandAugment baseline is incorrectly characterized as 'not completely zero-shot' while the proposed method benefits from similar compositionality. RandAugment applies random sequences of augmentations during training, meaning it implicitly sees combinations like rotation + darkening—precisely the types of shifts the paper evaluates. In contrast, the proposed method trains on fixed, hand-selected augmentations, which may be less robust. This weakens the claimed advantage in Table 4. A fair zero-shot evaluation would require both methods to avoid any exposure to the target augmentation type. The authors should clarify the evaluation protocol and consider a stricter split where neither method observes the specific augmentation or its components.

- Lookahead search lacks reproducibility and theoretical grounding: The lookahead grouping method, adapted from multi-task learning, is presented as a key contribution, but its implementation details are insufficient for replication. Figure 2 shows performance differences after gradient steps, but the paper does not specify how many steps are evaluated, whether updates are scaled, or how conflicting signals (e.g., bright vs. dark) are resolved. Moreover, the observation that vertical flip causes performance drops across the board raises questions about whether this is a property of the augmentation or an optimization artifact (e.g., large gradient norms). Without ablation on the lookahead hyperparameters or a comparison to simpler grouping strategies (e.g., clustering by feature divergence), the method's value is unclear. The authors should report sensitivity to step size and horizon, and test whether random or diversity-based groupings achieve similar results.

- Evaluation metrics fail to capture distribution diversity or difficulty: The Average Distribution Score is a simple mean of accuracies across augmentations, ignoring both the severity of each shift and the relationships between them. For example, Dark and Bright are likely closer in feature space than Jigsaw and Rotation, yet they are treated as equally distinct. This allows performance on easy augmentations to mask failure on harder ones. The paper acknowledges this in Section 4.3 but does not act on it: no distance-weighted metric is evaluated, and no analysis links score improvements to actual distribution separation. As a result, the claim of 'diversity' in generalization is unsupported. The authors should compute pairwise distances in representation space (e.g., using a pretrained model) and report a diversity-weighted score to validate their metric proposal.

- Limited validation on real-world shifts undermines practical relevance: All experiments use synthetic augmentations on CIFAR-10, with no evaluation on established benchmarks like WILDS or ImageNet-C that reflect real-world distribution shifts. While geometric and color augmentations are convenient, they may not capture the complexity of domain shifts in wildlife monitoring or medical imaging. The paper cites WILDS but does not test on it, leaving open whether the proposed grouping strategy generalizes beyond controlled corruptions. Without such validation, the method risks being perceived as a synthetic benchmark solution rather than a deployable approach. The authors should include at least one experiment on a real-world shift dataset, or clearly frame the work as a proof-of-concept limited to corruption-style shifts.

- Missing ablation on data overlap and its confounding effects: The paper notes in Section 3.3 that it tested grouping with and without data repetition, but it does not report the magnitude of the effect or how it influences affinity patterns. If models benefit from shared examples across augmentations (e.g., seeing the same image rotated and flipped), then the observed 'affinity' may reflect data overlap rather than true generalization. This is especially concerning for Jigsaw and Rotation, which could preserve semantic content. The authors should quantify the impact of data overlap by reporting performance differences between overlapping and disjoint splits, and clarify whether grouping success stems from shared instances or genuine transfer.

- No real-world distribution shift validation: The paper claims its method improves generalization to distribution shifts but only tests on synthetic augmentations of CIFAR-10. Readers will wonder whether the approach translates to real-world domain shifts like those in WILDS or ImageNet-C. To establish practical relevance, the authors should evaluate the proposed grouping strategy on at least one real-world benchmark—such as Camelyon17 from WILDS or ImageNet-C—using the same lookahead grouping and zero-shot inference protocol. Without this, the contribution risks being seen as a corruption-specific result rather than a general solution.

- Unverified claim of zero-shot distribution inference: The paper claims success in zero-shot distribution inference but uses a cross-validation setup where the model trains on three of four augmentations and tests on the held-out one. However, it never shows that performance on the held-out distribution is better than chance or a no-transfer baseline. A critical test is missing: compute the zero-shot accuracy gain of the grouped model over a model trained on unrelated augmentations (e.g., Bright, Cutout, Gamma) when both are tested on Rotation. This would isolate whether affinity-based grouping actually enables transfer, or if performance is driven by augmentation difficulty.

- No ablation on grouping strategy effectiveness: The paper asserts that lookahead search identifies high-affinity groups but does not compare it to simpler alternatives. A natural question is whether the improvement comes from the algorithm itself or merely from reducing the number of distributions. The authors should test a random grouping of four augmentations and a diversity-based grouping (e.g., maximizing pairwise dissimilarity in feature space) under the same training budget and report Average Distribution Scores. If these baselines perform similarly, the lookahead method’s added complexity is not justified.

- Missing analysis of why vertical flip disrupts training: Figure 2 shows that gradient updates on vertical flip (UD Flip) cause widespread performance drops, yet the paper does not investigate why. This matters because if the issue is due to large gradient norms or label noise, it could be fixed with minor changes rather than avoided entirely. The authors should compute the norm of gradients during updates on UD Flip versus other augmentations and test whether clipping or down-weighting it eliminates the negative effect. Without this, the exclusion of vertical flip appears heuristic rather than principled.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Define 'data distribution' consistently and distinguish between semantic, geometric, and photometric shifts in both framing and evaluation.

- Re-evaluate zero-shot claims with a fair protocol that prevents both the proposed method and RandAugment from seeing components of the target augmentation.

- Provide full implementation details of the lookahead search and include ablation on its sensitivity to hyperparameters and comparison to simpler grouping baselines.

- Replace or augment the Average Distribution Score with a diversity-weighted metric using feature-space distances between distributions.

- Validate the approach on at least one real-world distribution shift benchmark (e.g., WILDS or ImageNet-C) to support practical relevance.

comments.title: Misleading claim about eliminating distribution shift
comments.quote: We first illustrate the impact of adding the test distribution to the training distribution, such that it is no longer a Distribution Shift.
comments.explanation: Including a specific test-time augmentation in training reduces domain mismatch for that condition, but does not eliminate distribution shift. The shift arises from a change in the data-generating process, not merely from the absence of a transformation during training. Framing alignment through augmentation as 'removing' distribution shift overstates the result and misrepresents the problem. Replace the sentence with: 'We evaluate the benefit of including a specific test-time augmentation in training, which reduces the domain gap for that condition.'

comments.title: Incorrect attribution of texture bias finding
comments.quote: *Ovadia et al. (2019)* utilized style randomization to understand intrinsic properties of Convolutional Neural Networks, finding a texture bias.
comments.explanation: The discovery that ImageNet-trained CNNs are biased toward texture was established by Geirhos et al. (2018) using Stylized-ImageNet, not by Ovadia et al. (2019). Ovadia et al. evaluated model robustness under distribution shifts, including style transfer, but did not claim to identify the texture bias. This misattribution risks misleading readers about a foundational result in vision. Correct the sentence to: 'Geirhos et al. (2018) showed that CNNs exhibit a texture bias using stylized images; Ovadia et al. (2019) later evaluated model robustness under such shifts.'

comments.title: Misleading justification for RandAugment baseline
comments.quote: Although RandAugment has never seen any of these distributions in isolation, it has seen compositions of them such as rotation, then translation, then darker applied to a single image. Thus, the RandAugment baseline is not truly Zero-Shot.
comments.explanation: This argument applies equally to the proposed method: training on Translate, Darker, and Jigsaw also exposes the model to composite transformations. The paper does not show that its grouping strategy avoids compositionality more than RandAugment. Claiming RandAugment is 'not truly Zero-Shot' while implying the proposed method is, creates a double standard. Remove the justification and instead clarify that both methods involve exposure to related shifts, making the comparison one of grouping efficacy rather than zero-shot purity.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of data distribution via augmentation
comments.quote: We simulate the different distributions through Data Augmentations. We then scale this up to N=10 distributions in training.
comments.explanation: The term 'data distribution' is used to refer to deterministic image transformations like Rotation or Jigsaw, but this conflates procedural augmentations with statistical distributions. A data distribution is a probability measure over inputs and labels, not a fixed transformation. Without showing that each augmentation induces a measurable change in input density or class-conditional distribution, the claim that the model learns 'N=10 distributions' is misleading. Rewrite to clarify: 'We apply image transformations that induce distinct input corruptions, which we treat as proxy distribution shifts.'

comments.title: Unjustified grouping choice despite data overlap confound
comments.quote: From Figure 2, we propose to group Rotation, Translation, Dark, and Jigsaw to form our subset. In order to further understand the impact of a gradient update on one distribution to the performance of another, we avoid repeating data.
comments.explanation: The authors select a specific group based on Figure 2, which uses overlapping data, and justify it by noting a 'similar grouping analysis' without repetition. However, they do not confirm that the same four-augmentation group emerges in the disjoint-data setting. 'Similar analysis' could mean similar patterns of interference without the same optimal subset. This weakens the rationale for the chosen group. To support the claim, the authors should explicitly report whether Rotation, Translation, Dark, and Jigsaw are identified as high-affinity in the no-repetition case.

comments.title: Misleading indexing of UD Flip contradicts figure and text
comments.quote: The augmentations are indexed according to their position in the following list: Rotate = 0, Translate = 1, Crop = 2, Cutout = 3, Bright = 4, Dark = 5, Gamma Contrast = 6, LR Flip = 7, UD Flip = 8, and Jigsaw = 9. We find a strong outlier result where a proposed gradient update on Vertical Flipping (UD Flip) causes a significant drop in performance across the other augmentations.
comments.explanation: The text assigns UD Flip to index 8, implying it corresponds to the 9th row in Figure 2. However, visual inspection shows that the row with strong negative transfer across columns—especially to Bright, Dark, and Gamma—is the 7th row, which aligns with index 6 (Gamma Contrast), not 8. The 9th row (index 8) shows only mild negative effects. This discrepancy undermines the central observation that UD Flip causes widespread performance drops. Either the indexing or the figure must be corrected to align with the empirical result.

comments.title: Inconsistent handling of Darker in results
comments.quote: However, the other 3 clusters outperform the 10 Augmentation scheme other than the Jigsaw test. We find an interesting division between distributions that benefit from specialization (Rotation and Translation) and others that benefit from the ensemble (Darker and Jigsaw).
comments.explanation: The text claims Darker benefits from the ensemble, yet earlier it states that leaving out Darker causes optimization difficulties and very low accuracy. This suggests Darker is critical for stability, not that it passively benefits from being in the group. The phrasing implies symmetry where the evidence points to asymmetry. Rewrite as: 'We find an interesting division between distributions that benefit from specialization (Rotation and Translation) and others that are critical for stability (Darker) or benefit from the ensemble (Jigsaw).'

comments.title: Contradictory performance claims across tables
comments.quote: We encountered an optimization difficulty with the Rotate, Translate, Jigsaw (abbreviated as RTJ in Table3) that achieves low performance. However, the other 3 clusters outperform the 10 Augmentation scheme other than the Jigsaw test.
comments.explanation: The first sentence states RTJ has low performance, implying poor results for that group. The second claims the other three clusters outperform the 10 Augmentation baseline—yet RTJ is one of the four clusters. This creates a logical inconsistency unless the remaining groups are exceptionally strong, which is not quantified. Either RTJ underperforms but the average still exceeds the baseline (plausible), or the claim of outperformance needs qualification. Rewrite as: 'Despite poor performance in the RTJ group, three of the four clusters achieve higher scores than the 10 Augmentation scheme, except on the Jigsaw test.'

comments.title: Misleading zero-shot inference comparison in Table 4
comments.quote:
|  3 Augmentations | 72.4% | 71.9% | 45.5% | 65.2% | 62.6%  |
|  RandAugment | 57.4% | 49.7% | 82.2% | 69.5% | 72.6%  |
|  No Augmentation | 46.5% | 35.6% | 68.1% | 62.5% | 54.2%  |

Table 4: Zero-Shot Distribution Inference.
comments.explanation: Table 4 compares the proposed method to RandAugment under a zero-shot inference setup, but RandAugment applies random compositions of augmentations during training—including potentially the test ones. If Gamma Contrast is in its search space, its 72.6% performance is not zero-shot. This invalidates the comparison: the proposed method may appear worse not due to inferior transfer, but because it adheres to a stricter protocol. Either restrict RandAugment to a search space disjoint from test distributions or relabel the experiment as partial-shot evaluation.

comments.title: Unsupported claim about CycleGAN and DALL-E enabling domain generalization
comments.quote: believe techniques such as CycleGAN Zhu et al. (2017) and DALL-E Ramesh et al. (2021) will allow us to control Domain Generalization with Data Augmentation-style interfaces. For example, the iWildlife-monitoring camera in the WILDS benchmark encounters a new location for a wildelife monitoring camera at test time. A CycleGAN may be able to map the existing training data into this new location, further guided with text interfaces from DALL-E. We propose that we can train with this inferred novel distribution to perform better in the Distribution Shift.
comments.explanation: The proposal assumes CycleGAN can learn a mapping from training to a novel test location without paired data, which contradicts its requirement for cycle consistency. DALL-E is a text-to-image generator, not a domain adaptation tool, and using it to 'guide' image translation lacks technical grounding. The claim that generated images preserve ecological semantics without introducing misleading artifacts is speculative. Either remove the claim or reframe it as a hypothesis requiring validation, noting the significant challenges in unpaired domain translation for real-world shifts.

comments.title: Misleading analogy between distribution shift and few-shot learning
comments.quote: Few-Shot Learning describes learning a new task with a few examples, hopefully achieved by leveraging information from previously learned tasks. Zero-Shot Learning refers to a more extreme case in which the model performs a new task, but no additional learning is allowed. We can similarly view Distribution Shifts as having a few examples from the new distribution or having to perform Zero-Shot task inference.
comments.explanation: Distribution shift preserves the task (same labels, same objective) while changing input marginals; few-shot and zero-shot learning involve generalization to new tasks or classes. The analogy incorrectly implies that seeing a few examples from a new domain is equivalent to few-shot learning, which misrepresents standard usage. Rewrite to clarify: 'We can frame distribution shift as a zero-shot domain generalization problem, where the model must adapt to a new data distribution without labeled examples from that domain.'

comments.title: Ambiguous use of 'Zero-Shot task inference'
comments.quote: We can similarly view Distribution Shifts as having a few examples from the new distribution or having to perform Zero-Shot task inference.
comments.explanation: The term 'Zero-Shot task inference' is inappropriate because the task does not change under distribution shift—only the input distribution does. True zero-shot learning involves unseen classes or output spaces. Using 'task inference' here risks confusion with established terminology. Replace with precise language: 'We can frame distribution shift as a zero-shot domain generalization problem, where the model must adapt to a new data distribution without labeled examples from that domain.'

comments.title: Unclear weighting scheme for proposed metric
comments.quote: We intend to weight the average accuracies by the number of evaluated distributions and the distance between these distributions.
comments.explanation: Weighting by 'the number of evaluated distributions' and 'the distance between them' is logically inconsistent. The former normalizes for group size, the latter emphasizes diversity. These serve different purposes and cannot be jointly applied without clarification. Moreover, 'weighting by the number of distributions' is ambiguous—does it mean dividing by the count or multiplying? Define a single coherent rule, e.g., 'weighted by inverse pairwise distances to emphasize generalization across dissimilar distributions,' and provide the full formula.

comments.title: Circular dependency in score definition
comments.quote: A strong Distribution Generalization Score should also be correlated with performance on Zero-Shot Distribution Inference.
comments.explanation: This creates a circular validation: the proposed score is deemed 'strong' if it correlates with zero-shot inference, which is itself a primary outcome of the method. This makes correlation expected rather than informative. A meaningful validation would link the score to an independent measure, such as robustness on unseen real-world shifts. Remove the sentence and instead define the score based on measurable properties of distribution separation, independent of downstream tasks that are methodologically dependent.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes a framework for learning multiple data distributions simultaneously using data augmentations to prepare for distribution shift. The authors experiment with training models on up to 10 augmented distributions, develop a grouping method using lookahead search, and evaluate zero-shot generalization to held-out distributions. While the empirical approach is interesting, the paper has significant methodological limitations in how it simulates and evaluates distribution shifts.

The paper presents a practical empirical investigation into multi-distribution learning using data augmentations. The authors demonstrate clear performance improvements when training on specific test distributions and show the challenges of scaling to many distributions. The adaptation of the lookahead search algorithm from multi-task learning is a clever approach for identifying compatible distributions. However, the reliance on simple geometric augmentations as proxies for real-world distribution shifts raises questions about the practical relevance of the findings.

Overview issues:

- Limited realism of augmentation-based distribution shifts: The paper treats simple geometric and color augmentations (rotations, flips, brightness changes) as representative of real-world distribution shifts. This is a significant limitation because real distribution shifts in applications like wildlife monitoring or medical imaging involve far more complex changes - different lighting conditions, camera angles, backgrounds, and domain changes. The authors acknowledge this in the future work section but do not address how well their findings on simple augmentations would transfer to more realistic shifts. The experiments using ImageNet-C and WILDS as motivation create a mismatch between the motivating problem (complex real-world shifts) and the actual evaluation (simple augmentations).

- Questionable zero-shot evaluation protocol: The zero-shot distribution inference evaluation in Table 4 has a methodological flaw. When holding out one distribution (e.g., Rotation) and training on the others (Translate, Darker, Jigsaw), the model has still seen the base CIFAR-10 images multiple times through different augmentations. This means the 'zero-shot' evaluation is not truly zero-shot - the model has exposure to the underlying data distribution. A more rigorous evaluation would use completely held-out test sets or more distinct distribution shifts. Additionally, the comparison to RandAugment as 'not truly zero-shot' is inconsistent - if RandAugment has seen compositions of augmentations, then the grouped training approach has also seen the base images multiple times, just in different ways.

- Insufficient analysis of grouping method scalability: The lookahead search for grouping distributions appears computationally expensive, requiring pairwise comparisons of gradient updates across all distributions. The paper mentions this takes roughly 20 hours for 10 distributions on an A100 GPU, but does not analyze how this scales to larger numbers of distributions or more complex augmentations. For practical deployment, the computational cost of this grouping step could be prohibitive. The authors should provide complexity analysis or demonstrate the method on a larger scale to establish its feasibility. Without this, the grouping approach remains a proof-of-concept rather than a practical solution.

- Underdeveloped distribution generalization score: The proposed 'Average Distribution Score' is simply the mean accuracy across test distributions, which the authors themselves acknowledge is naive in Section 4.3. They propose developing a more sophisticated metric that weights by distribution count and diversity, but this remains undeveloped. This is problematic because the score is used to evaluate and compare different approaches throughout the paper. Without a well-justified metric that accounts for distribution diversity and difficulty, the reported improvements may be misleading. For example, achieving high scores on easy distributions (flips) while failing on harder ones (rotations) could still yield a decent average score.

- Missing ablation of key methodological choices: The paper lacks critical ablation studies. There is no analysis of how performance varies with different numbers of epochs for the grouped approach versus training on all distributions. The choice of alternating training every epoch versus other schedules is not justified or explored. The impact of the specific augmentations chosen (e.g., why these 10 particular ones?) is not analyzed. Most importantly, there is no comparison to simpler baselines like training on random subsets of distributions or using curriculum learning approaches. These ablations are necessary to understand whether the lookahead search grouping is truly providing value over simpler heuristics.

- Missing demonstration on realistic distribution shifts: The paper's central claim is about learning multiple data distributions to handle distribution shift, yet all experiments use simple geometric augmentations on CIFAR-10. This creates a disconnect between the motivation (real-world shifts like those in WILDS) and the actual evaluation. The paper needs at least one experiment demonstrating the framework on a more realistic distribution shift, such as the ImageNet-C benchmark or a subset of WILDS datasets. Without this, readers cannot assess whether the grouping method and zero-shot inference would work beyond toy augmentations. A concrete experiment would be to apply the lookahead grouping to the 15 corruption types in ImageNet-C and show whether the identified groups generalize to held-out corruptions.

- No worked example of distribution affinity computation: The paper introduces the lookahead search for computing distribution affinities but never provides a concrete, worked example of this computation. Readers cannot verify or understand how the affinity scores are calculated from the gradient updates. The paper should include a step-by-step computation for a pair of distributions (e.g., Rotation and Translation), showing the exact formulation of the performance change metric and how it's evaluated. This would help readers assess the validity of the grouping method and potentially implement it themselves.

- Unvalidated zero-shot inference claims: The paper claims to demonstrate zero-shot distribution inference but the evaluation protocol is questionable. When holding out one distribution, the model has still seen the base images through other augmentations. The paper needs a truly zero-shot experiment where the test distribution contains images not seen in any form during training. For instance, they could use a different dataset as the held-out distribution or apply a novel transformation not present in training. Without this validation, the zero-shot claims remain unconvincing, especially compared to the RandAugment baseline which the authors criticize as 'not truly zero-shot'.

- Missing implementation guidance for grouping method: The paper presents a lookahead search for grouping distributions but provides no practical guidance on implementation or computational requirements. The method appears to require training a model on each distribution separately to compute gradient updates, which would be prohibitively expensive for large-scale problems. The paper needs to discuss implementation details such as: (1) the exact algorithmic steps and pseudocode, (2) computational complexity analysis, (3) whether the affinities can be computed efficiently without full training, and (4) how the method scales with the number of distributions. Without this, readers cannot determine if the approach is feasible for real applications.

Recommendation: Major revision. The paper presents an interesting empirical framework for multi-distribution learning, but has significant methodological limitations that must be addressed. The augmentation-based simulation of distribution shifts lacks realism, the zero-shot evaluation protocol is flawed, and key components like the grouping method and evaluation metric need more rigorous analysis and validation.

Revision targets:

- Add experiments with more realistic distribution shifts (e.g., using CycleGAN translations or WILDS domains) to validate that findings on simple augmentations transfer to practical scenarios

- Redesign the zero-shot evaluation to use truly held-out test distributions or clarify the distinction between the proposed approach and RandAugment baseline

- Include computational complexity analysis of the lookahead search and demonstrate scalability to larger numbers of distributions

- Develop and validate the proposed distribution generalization score with proper weighting for distribution diversity and difficulty

- Add comprehensive ablation studies including training schedules, baseline grouping strategies, and analysis of augmentation choices

comments.title: Incorrect citation attribution for texture bias
comments.quote: [Ovadia et al. (2019)] utilized style randomization to understand intrinsic properties of Convolutional Neural Networks, finding a texture bias.
comments.explanation: The citation attribution is incorrect. The texture bias finding using style randomization is from Geirhos et al. (2019) in their paper 'ImageNet-trained CNNs are biased towards texture rather than shape', not Ovadia et al. (2019). Ovadia et al. (2019) focused on uncertainty estimation techniques under distribution shift. This error misrepresents the contribution of the cited work and should be corrected to properly credit the texture bias discovery.

comments.title: Table 3 calculation error in RTD average
comments.explanation: The Average Distribution Score for the RTD row appears to be calculated incorrectly. The row shows 71.9%, 71.0%, and 81.4% for the three test distributions. The average of these three values is (71.9 + 71.0 + 81.4)/3 = 74.77%, which rounds to 74.8%. However, this calculation assumes the average should only include the non-N/A values. If the Average Distribution Score is meant to be computed across all four test distributions (treating N/A as 0%), the score would be (71.9 + 71.0 + 81.4 + 0)/4 = 56.08%, not 74.8%. The paper needs to clarify the exact formula for computing the Average Distribution Score when some test distributions are marked as N/A.

comments.title: Inconsistent zero-shot evaluation methodology
comments.quote:
|  Training Distributions | Rotate Test | Translate Test | Darker Test | Jigsaw Test | Gamma Contrast Test  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  3 Augmentations | 72.4% | 71.9% | 45.5% | 65.2% | 62.6%  |
comments.explanation: The zero-shot evaluation in Table 4 contains a logical inconsistency. The paper claims this is 'Zero-Shot Distribution Inference' where Gamma Contrast Test is the held-out distribution. However, the '3 Augmentations' row shows 72.4% on Rotate Test, 71.9% on Translate Test, and 65.2% on Jigsaw Test. These high scores suggest the model was trained on these distributions, contradicting the zero-shot premise. If this is truly zero-shot evaluation, these should be much lower, closer to the 'No Augmentation' baseline. The table appears to be showing in-distribution performance rather than zero-shot performance.

comments.title: Contradiction in proposed weighting scheme
comments.quote: We intend to weight the average accuracies by the number of evaluated distributions and the distance between these distributions.
comments.explanation: The proposed weighting scheme contains a logical contradiction. Weighting by the number of evaluated distributions would give equal weight to each distribution regardless of how many are being evaluated, which is the same as a simple average. If you have N distributions and weight each accuracy by 1/N, you get the arithmetic mean. The text suggests this is an improvement over the current simple average, but mathematically it's identical. The authors should either remove the 'number of evaluated distributions' factor or clarify how this differs from their current approach.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about data repetition impact
comments.quote: We find a similar grouping analysis with and without repeating data.
comments.explanation: The authors claim they find similar grouping analysis with and without repeating data, but this statement lacks quantitative support. The text mentions that Figure 3 illustrates the lookahead updates when data is split to avoid repetition, but no specific comparison metrics or similarity measures are provided. Without concrete evidence (e.g., correlation coefficients between the two analyses, specific differences in affinity scores, or quantitative measures of how the groupings change), readers cannot assess whether 'similar' is meaningful or if the differences are actually significant for the grouping decisions.

comments.title: Missing justification for proposed grouping
comments.quote: From Figure 2, we propose to group Rotation, Translation, Dark, and Jigsaw to form our subset.
comments.explanation: The authors propose grouping Rotation (0), Translation (1), Dark (5), and Jigsaw (9) based on Figure 2, but they don't explain the selection criteria. Given that Figure 2 shows the impact of gradient updates across all distribution pairs, the rationale for choosing these specific four distributions is unclear. Are they chosen because they have minimal negative impact on each other? Because they form a cluster in the affinity space? Or based on some other criterion? Without explaining the decision rule for selecting this subset from the full affinity matrix, readers cannot understand why this particular grouping was chosen or evaluate whether it's optimal.

comments.title: Conceptual confusion between task and distribution inference
comments.quote: We can similarly view Distribution Shifts as having a few examples from the new distribution or having to perform Zero-Shot task inference.
comments.explanation: The passage conflates two distinct concepts: distribution shift adaptation and zero-shot task inference. Distribution shift involves adapting to changes in data distribution within the same task (e.g., same classification labels but different image characteristics), while zero-shot task inference involves performing entirely new tasks (e.g., new label sets or problem types). The paper's own experiments focus on distribution shifts through augmentations, not new tasks. This conceptual mixing creates confusion about what the zero-shot evaluation actually measures.

comments.title: Overgeneralization about data augmentations
comments.quote: Regardless of the particular type of generalization, we can likely form Data Augmentations that communicate expected Distribution Shifts.
comments.explanation: This claim is too strong and not well-supported. While data augmentations can simulate some types of distribution shifts (like geometric transformations or color changes), they cannot adequately represent many real-world distribution shifts mentioned earlier in the text, such as those in the WILDS benchmark. For example, domain shifts in wildlife monitoring involve changes in camera angles, lighting conditions, and backgrounds that cannot be captured by simple augmentations. The statement should be qualified to acknowledge the limitations of augmentations for certain types of shifts.

comments.title: Flawed RandAugment zero-shot argument
comments.quote: Although RandAugment has never seen any of these distributions in isolation, it has seen compositions of them such as rotation, then translation, then darker applied to a single image. Thus, the RandAugment baseline is not truly Zero-Shot.
comments.explanation: The argument against RandAugment being truly zero-shot is logically inconsistent. If RandAugment has seen compositions that include rotation, translation, and darker augmentations, then by the same logic, the grouped training approach is also not truly zero-shot when it trains on multiple distributions. The model sees the base CIFAR-10 images through multiple different augmentations in both cases. The authors should either acknowledge this inconsistency or provide a clearer distinction between what constitutes 'truly zero-shot' in their evaluation framework.

comments.title: Inconsistent table caption formatting
comments.quote: Table 1: The efficacy of training with a specialized distribution. The bolded values indicate the maximum accuracy achieved on each data distribution. The bolded values indicate the maximum accuracy for each data distribution.
comments.explanation: The caption for Table 1 contains a redundant sentence that repeats the same information twice. The second sentence 'The bolded values indicate the maximum accuracy for each data distribution' is identical to the first sentence except for minor wording. This appears to be a copy-paste error that should be corrected for clarity.

comments.title: Undefined abbreviation in table
comments.explanation: The table entries 'Seq-RJ', 'Seq-JR', 'Alt-RJ', and 'Alt-SH-RJ' use abbreviations that are not defined in the table caption or explained before the table appears. While the text later explains 'Seq' and 'Alt', the specific combinations like 'RJ' (Rotation-Jigsaw) and 'JR' (Jigsaw-Rotation) are never explicitly defined. Readers must infer these meanings from context. Add a footnote or brief explanation in the caption defining these abbreviations.

comments.title: Gamma Contrast description is mathematically vague
comments.quote: The Gamma Contrast augmentation alters the distribution of pixel values to a smaller window between 0 and 255.
comments.explanation: This description of Gamma Contrast is mathematically imprecise and potentially misleading. Gamma correction doesn't 'alter the distribution to a smaller window' - it applies a non-linear transformation to pixel values using the formula output = input^gamma. For gamma > 1, it darkens the image; for gamma < 1, it brightens it. The pixel values still span the full 0-255 range, just with a different distribution. The current description suggests the range is compressed, which is incorrect.

comments.title: Jigsaw augmentation description lacks implementation details
comments.quote: The Jigsaw augmentation divides the image into n pixel windows and shuffles their location in the image.
comments.explanation: The Jigsaw augmentation description is incomplete because it doesn't specify the critical parameter of grid size. 'n pixel windows' is ambiguous - does this mean n total windows (e.g., 3x3 grid = 9 windows) or windows of size n×n pixels? The implementation details matter because the difficulty of the jigsaw task depends heavily on the grid granularity. Without specifying whether they use 2x2, 3x3, or another grid size, readers cannot reproduce the experiment or assess the complexity of this distribution shift.

comments.title: Figure 2 incorrectly labeled as table
comments.quote: Figure 2: This table shows the performance difference by taking the proposed gradient update on the y-axis on the test distributions on the x-axis.
comments.explanation: The caption refers to Figure 2 as 'This table' when it is clearly a figure. This creates confusion about what is being presented. The caption should be corrected to refer to it as a figure consistently throughout.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper investigates Multi-Distribution Learning (MDL) using data augmentation as a proxy for distribution shifts. The authors propose a framework to group distributions based on their training affinity using a lookahead search algorithm and evaluate performance through zero-shot inference.

The paper tackles an important problem in distribution shift with an intuitive, practical approach. While the empirical results on CIFAR-10 are interesting, the methodology lacks a rigorous theoretical grounding to justify the distribution grouping strategy, and the scalability of the approach remains unproven outside of small-scale synthetic augmentations.

Overview issues:

- Lack of Validation on Real-World Distribution Shifts: The paper relies entirely on synthetic data augmentations (e.g., rotation, jigsaw, brightness) to simulate distribution shifts. While these provide a controlled environment, they do not capture the semantic or structural complexity of real-world domain shifts like those found in the WILDS benchmark. The authors acknowledge this limitation in the discussion but do not provide a bridge to demonstrate that the learned affinities (e.g., Rotation and Jigsaw) hold for actual domain adaptation tasks. To improve the paper's impact, the authors should evaluate their grouping strategy on at least one real-world dataset from the WILDS suite to show that the proposed affinity metrics are not artifacts of the specific augmentation set used.

- Unclear Theoretical Basis for Lookahead Affinity: In Section 3.3, the authors adapt the Task Groupings algorithm from Fifty et al. (2021) to identify distributions that train well together. However, the paper does not provide a theoretical justification for why a single gradient lookahead step is a sufficient proxy for long-term multi-distribution convergence. The results in Figure 2 show strong negative affinities for certain pairs, but it is unclear if these are inherent to the distribution geometry or merely a consequence of the specific learning rate and architecture chosen. A more robust analysis would include a sensitivity study of the lookahead step size or a comparison with other metrics like Fisher Information Matrix alignment to confirm the grouping's validity.

- Confounding Factors in Distribution Grouping: The authors attempt to address the issue of repeating data by partitioning the CIFAR-10 set, yet the resulting performance gains are modest and the grouping remains largely similar. There is a significant concern that the observed affinities are driven by the low-level visual statistics of the augmentations rather than the underlying distribution shift. Because the paper does not quantify the 'distance' between these distributions beyond a naive average, it is difficult to interpret whether the model is learning generalizable features or simply overfitting to the specific noise patterns of the augmentations. Adding a formal measure of distribution diversity or distance, as suggested in Section 4.3, is essential to validate that the grouping strategy is actually capturing meaningful task relationships.

- Scalability and Computational Overhead: The paper notes that learning 10 distributions simultaneously is computationally expensive and yields poor results, necessitating the lookahead search for smaller subsets. However, the search process itself is computationally intensive, requiring multiple training runs to evaluate affinity. The paper fails to provide a clear analysis of the total compute budget required to find the optimal grouping versus the performance gains achieved. A more comprehensive evaluation would compare the total training time of the 'grouped' approach against a standard ensemble or a larger, single-model baseline to demonstrate a clear Pareto improvement in efficiency.

- Absence of a formal definition for Distribution Affinity: The paper relies on the Task Groupings algorithm to identify 'affinities' between distributions, yet it never formally defines what affinity represents in terms of the model's loss landscape or gradient geometry. While the lookahead search provides an empirical proxy, it remains an opaque heuristic. To ground this contribution, the authors should define affinity mathematically—for instance, as the cosine similarity between gradient updates on different distributions—and show whether this metric correlates with the observed performance gains in Table 3. Without this, the 'affinity' concept lacks the theoretical rigor required to justify why specific groupings are superior to others.

- Lack of empirical validation for the proposed Distribution Generalization Metrics: The abstract and introduction promise a new framework for Distribution Generalization Metrics, yet the empirical results rely solely on naive average accuracy. The paper acknowledges this as a limitation but fails to demonstrate how their proposed weighted metric would actually behave in practice. The authors should compute their proposed metric for the results in Table 3 and show that it provides a more consistent ranking of models than average accuracy, specifically by demonstrating that it penalizes models that overfit to a subset of the distributions while rewarding those that cover a diverse range.

- Missing sensitivity analysis for the Lookahead search hyperparameters: The Lookahead search is central to the paper's methodology, yet the results are presented as if the grouping is invariant to the search configuration. It is unclear if the identified 'high affinity' clusters are robust to variations in the lookahead step size or the specific optimizer settings. The authors should provide a plot showing how the affinity matrix in Figure 2 changes under different lookahead learning rates or batch sizes. This would clarify whether the grouping strategy is a property of the data distributions themselves or merely a byproduct of the optimization trajectory.

Recommendation: major revision. The paper provides an interesting empirical framework but lacks the necessary rigor in validating its core claims about distribution affinity and generalization. It needs to demonstrate that the proposed grouping strategy generalizes to non-synthetic shifts and provide a more principled justification for the lookahead approach.

Revision targets:

- Include an evaluation on a real-world distribution shift benchmark (e.g., WILDS) to validate the grouping strategy.

- Perform a sensitivity analysis on the lookahead search hyperparameters to demonstrate the stability of the identified distribution groupings.

- Add a formal definition or metric for distribution distance to explain why certain distributions have high or low affinity.

- Provide a clear computational budget analysis comparing the lookahead search overhead to the performance gains of the resulting model.

comments.title: Logical inconsistency in distribution shift definition
comments.quote: We first illustrate the impact of adding the test distribution to the training distribution, such that it is no longer a Distribution Shift.
comments.explanation: The authors define distribution shift as a discrepancy between training and test distributions, but then claim that adding the test distribution to the training set eliminates the shift. This is logically flawed because if the test distribution is included in the training set, the model is no longer performing distribution generalization; it is performing standard empirical risk minimization on a union of distributions. The claim that this 'is no longer a Distribution Shift' confuses the problem of generalization under shift with the trivial case of training on the target distribution. Please clarify that this is a control experiment to measure an upper bound of performance, rather than a method to combat distribution shift itself.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of the Zero-Shot Distribution Inference experiment
comments.quote: We find much better performance with this cluster compared to a strong RandAugment baseline, which is not completely Zero-Shot, as well as a weak baseline of a model trained without any Data Augmentation.
comments.explanation: The authors label their cross-validation experiment (training on 3 of 4 distributions and evaluating on the 4th) as 'Zero-Shot' inference. By definition, zero-shot inference implies the model has never seen the target distribution during training. Since the model is trained on a subset of augmentations and then evaluated on a held-out augmentation, this is a standard 'leave-one-out' cross-validation or 'domain generalization' task. Labeling this as zero-shot is misleading because the model is explicitly trained on data distributions that are structurally identical to the test distribution, just with different parameterizations of the same augmentation type.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Lookahead gradient update definition
comments.quote: We similarly compute the change in performance on the Translation distribution after taking a gradient step on the Rotated training set.
comments.explanation: The description of the lookahead mechanism is mathematically underspecified. The text refers to 'change in performance' without specifying whether this is a first-order approximation (gradient inner product) or a full second-order evaluation, nor does it define the learning rate or the specific loss function used. Without these, the 'affinity' between distributions is not well-defined. Please add a formal definition of the lookahead operation, such as ΔP_{i,j} = L_j(θ - α ∇_θ L_i(θ)) - L_j(θ), to clarify the metric used for grouping.

comments.title: Inconsistency regarding the definition of the Average Distribution Score
comments.quote: We measure our models using the Average Distribution Accuracy and propose a new framework of Distribution Generalization Metrics. These performance scores are a weighted average of the model loss or accuracy, the number of distributions it can cover, and the diversity of these distributions.
comments.explanation: The text defines the 'Distribution Generalization Metrics' as a weighted average involving distribution diversity and coverage.
